title: Using Machine Learning to Measure Conservatism
abstract: This study proposes an approach to measure conservatism using machine learning techniques that are not constrained by functional form restrictions. We extend the differential timeliness model to allow for observable characteristics related to conservatism to follow nonlinear relationships. By developing machine learning measures of conservatism, we draw attention to potential benefits and drawbacks and show how its insights complement conventional measures. Our broader goal is to investigate the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms for filtering noise in traditional archival studies and uncovering more complex empirical patterns.

author.Jeremy Bertomeu: Olin Business School, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri 63130
author.Edwige Cheynel: Olin Business School, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri 63130
author.Yifei Liao: Paul Merage School of Business, University of California, Irvine, California 92697
author.Mario Milone: Rady School of Management, University of California, San Diego, California 92093

article.1. Introduction: Conservatism is traditionally understood as a differential response of the accounting process to losses versus gains, as manifested by a greater level of verifiability required to record positive news (Watts 2003). The asymmetry is reflected empirically by a structural break in the properties of accounting numbers around favorable transactions. An extensive literature builds on this relationship to develop measures of conservatism from the differential timeliness of price and earnings (Basu 1997;Kothari et al. 2010;Ball et al. 2013a,b). In this literature, the statistical models have many determinants entering linearly into the specification and do not account for interactions that may help identify conservatism. This study offers an exploratory analysis of machine learning techniques to measure conservatism when lifting functional form restrictions unrelated to conservatism. Most existing applications of machine learning in accounting tend to focus on prediction (Perols et al. 2017,Bao et al. 2020,Ding et al. 2020,Bertomeu et al. 2021,Chen et al. 2022), which involves the use of current financial information to predict information observed at a future date (e.g., future earnings or write-offs) or an ongoing irregularity (e.g., Accounting and Auditing Enforcement Releases or material restatement). By contrast, we use machine learning for a distinct purpose: as a tool for semiparametric estimation, it enables us to model an unknown function of interest and capture a greater proportion of the informative variation within a sample. AsAthey (2018, p. 508)summarizes, “ML does not add much to questions about identification…, but rather yields great improvements when the goal is semiparametric estimation or when there are a large number of covariates relative to the number of observations.” This goal is different from prediction, which captures “the process of generating decision rules fornewobservations” (Krupa and Minutti-Meza 2022) and emphasizes out-of-sample accuracy to assess whether the model adequately removes specificities of the sample. Our objective is to use all the information in the sample to semiparametrically estimate an assumed relationship in the data generating process (the measure), which requires a different approach in selecting the model and assessing its performance.1Prior literature regresses earnings on returns and firm characteristics and further includes interaction terms to account for the impact on negative returns to test for the asymmetric timeliness of earnings. We expand a set of commonly used features plausibly related to conservatism and generalize the specification of these features into the model so that functional forms need not be linear.2In the augmented model, the direct effect of prices on earnings and the interaction term conditional on negative returns may take a nonlinear functional form. We further impose theoretical restrictions on these functions reflecting various doctrines anchoring income as a measurement of a change in “net wealth,” as, for example, in Hicksian income defined as the amount that can be recovered by shareholders while preserving existing capital (Bromwich et al. 2010) or, as summarized in the Financial Accounting Standards Board Concepts Statements No. 8, “Comprehensive income as defined is a return on financial capital.”3Specifically, the estimation requires income to be nondecreasing in equity returns conditional on the set of characteristics used in the model. Validating an explanatory model is conceptually more complex than in a predictive model as we cannot rely on out-of-sample accuracy, so we instead use multiple validation criteria.Shmueli (2010)recommends using goodness-of-fit, so we first evaluate the incremental fit of the machine learning models. Our findings, suggest that the largest increase in explanatory power is achieved by incorporating economic characteristics rather than nonlinearities. Although machine learning models capture more variation than linear models estimated by year, the largest gains in explanatory power come from adding more economic variables. For purposes of identifying a functional form for firm conservatism, the average coefficients in linear models estimated by year only explain a small portion of the variation; however, a machine learning model with a single functional form achieves a comparable, and even slightly greater, fit than linear models estimated by years, with fewer estimated coefficients. Although goodness-of-fit results may reflect that certain measures capture more empirical variation, they do not necessarily indicate a superior explanation if the statistical model is mis-specified or fails to capture the intended economic construct. Therefore, caution should be exercised when interpreting these results. To address this limitation, we consider two approaches to evaluate the differences between traditional and machine learning measures. Our first approach is to assume that the true data-generating process may be captured by one of the measures, but which measure is true is unknown to the researcher. Hence, a researcher having a sufficiently diffuse prior over existing methodologies may favor measures that are more robust to other measures being correct. A mis-specified machine learning model appears to keep an error relative to the true coefficient hovering at about half of its unconditional standard deviation against all models versus typically multiple standard deviations when linear models are used to estimate a process generated from another model with more economic variables or nonlinearities. However, consistent with our expectation, we verify that machine learning models provide no benefit over linear models when the true data-generating process is linear and includes fewer economic variables. The choice of approach should therefore depend on whether nonlinearities and interactions are crucial to the research question. Next, we evaluate the measures based on several presumed intuitive properties of conservatism, along the arguments inEttredge et al. (2012)andGivoly et al. (2007)that (i) conservatism is anchored in policies and economic factors expected to be highly persistent, and (ii) conservatism should increase after a detected misstatement. When examining the time series of average conservatism, conventional linear measures that estimate conservatism by year obscure trends due to rapid year-over-year changes. By contrast, machine learning measures reveal a consistent decline in conservatism over time. These findings align with a secular decline in concepts supporting income statement conservatism and a shift toward the asset-liability view (Zeff 1999,2013;Orthaus et al. 2023).4Although incorporating more economic factors or estimating by year enhances the model’s fit, it decreases persistence across all specifications, including those using machine learning. When the full set of economic factors is included, machine learning measures exhibit higher persistence than a comparable model estimated by year, but lower persistence than an index-like linear model estimated as a pooled regression. In the context of restatements, several measures tend to indicate an increase in conservatism in the periods following restatement announcements, consistent with prior literature. However, there are a few caveats to this observation. Our analysis of the entire sample from 2000 to 2019 using the classification of irregularities fromSrinivasan et al. (2015), shows that, for measures using few economic factors, there tends to be an insignificant association between conservatism and post-restatement periods. This is true for both machine learning and the conventional approach involving a linear model estimated by year. All measures with the full set of economic factors consistently show a positive association with conservatism across both linear and machine learning models. When estimated using an additional indicator variable for a post-restatement period, as inEttredge et al. (2012), all measures demonstrate a significant positive association, even with a reduced number of economic factors with the notable exception of the linear model estimated by year.
article.2. Literature Review: Our study links to a growing literature exploring whether advances in machine learning can improve accounting measurements. This literature observes that many constructs relevant to the accounting process have functional forms that are unknown a priori to researchers. Studies such asPerols (2011)andPerols et al. (2017)are among the first to introduce machine learning methods in accounting. Recent studies have also examined the use of machine learning for textual analysis (Li 2010,Frankel et al. 2016), to analyze audit quality (Hu et al. 2020,Yang et al. 2020), compute more accurate accruals (Ding et al. 2020), or improve the analysis of financial statements (Binz et al. 2022,Chen et al. 2022), to examine the evolution of the relevance of accounting (Barth et al. 2023), to gauge the importance of corporate governance (Gow et al. 2023), or to predict misstatements (Bao et al. 2020,Bertomeu et al. 2021).5This literature overwhelmingly focuses on using machine learning for prediction. We adopt a different perspective and use machine learning methods to separate signal from noise and to extract an economic characteristic of interest. Our objective is thus to show whether machine learning methods can be nested into commonly used measures of differential timeliness, and test for the ability of machine learning to incorporate a greater portion of informative variation into the measure.6 Our baseline specification draws heavily from the differential timeliness model inBasu (1997)andKhan and Watts (2009). Taking the example of an asset impairment, Basu observes that timely reporting of bad news is reflected into earnings when a sufficient loss is expected; by contrast, gains are delayed until the earnings process is complete, implying a flatter relation between earnings and gain expectations. Conservatism results in a broken symmetry around gains and losses, assessed with a linear regression of earnings on returns with an interaction in the form of an indicator variable for negative returns (“bad news”). The original specification ofBasu (1997)assumes that conservatism does not change over time, whereasKhan and Watts (2009)assume that the degree of asymmetry may vary within firm. Since then, various studies have proposed methodologies and discussions to reduce its bias; for example, the recent study byBadia et al. (2021)suggests incorporating interacted controls for volatility, investment optionality, and other risk factors related to expected returns (Ball et al. 2013b,Frankel et al. 2019), which could drive asymmetries unrelated to differential timeliness. Our implementation of the machine learning measures allows researchers to incorporate additional interactions into the model based on a preferred set of controls. We develop a measure that retains the broken symmetry around market news but otherwise does not impose a specific parametric form on its components.7 There are continuing debates about using asymmetric timeliness in the measurement of conservatism (Beaver and Ryan 2005,Ryan 2006,Givoly et al. 2007), and a machine learning algorithm is a statistical tool that does not, on its own, provide a new method of identification. Our machine learning measure should therefore be used in conjunction with measures that capture other implications of conservatism.Roychowdhury and Watts (2007)argue market-to-book (M/B) is complementary to asymmetric timeliness in that conservatism is not a single-dimensional property of measurement. M/B captures cumulative conservatism through systematic under-recognition of accounting assets relative to investor expectations. In addition, income statement and balance sheet conservatism may have different economic implications. For example, if a firm reduces the value of its assets, high balance sheet conservatism may be followed by low income statement conservatism if there is no further scope for impairments (Sunder et al. 2018).8The ability of the algorithm to allow for an asymmetric timeliness that varies with more covariates (including M/B) and in a nonlinear manner provides a more comprehensive, albeit certainly imperfect, empirical design to control for biases due to omitted factors.
article.3. Empirical Strategy: 3.1. Model and Neural Network We assume the following nonlinear model of earnings and prices: InBasu (1997), the model is estimated with a pooled time-series regression where all functions are constants and researchers may use firm-level regressions to estimatecby firm (the Basu coefficient). InKhan and Watts (2009), hereafter KW, the functions are linear in three features, size (total assets),M/B, and leverage (total debt to market value of equity), and estimated by year. These designs are special cases of this specification setting the featuresYitas firms’ fixed effects or a linear function of characteristics interacted with time fixed effects. Our objective is to estimateEquation (1)potentially including more features inYitand without assuming a linear functional form. Because a neural network is used to encode the restrictions from (1) into a more general structure, it is helpful to obtain a general understanding of how neural networks fit data.9A network is composed of perceptrons (also called nodes or neurons) connected to each other. Each perceptron is a mathematical function that receives a vector as input and returns a scalar. If we denotexthe input vector, a perceptron returns a scalar𝑧=Φ⁡(𝒘𝒙+𝑏),z=Φ⁡(wx+b),wherewis a vector of weights,bis a bias term, andΦ⁡(.)Φ⁡(.)is called the activation function. When connected in a network, the output value of a perceptron becomes a component of the input vector of the next perceptron. We estimateEquation (1)with a custom neural network whose structure is illustrated inFigure 1. The input layer of the network is a vector of dimensionn+ 2 where the firstninputs correspond to the variablesYit, and the last two inputs are the returnRitand a dummy variableDitequal to one when the returnRitis negative. The square nodes in the figure correspond to embedded neural networks illustrated inFigure 2,(a)and(b), whose outputs are the values of the functionsf,g,g+c, andd. The output layer is a single perceptron whose inputs are the values of the functions andRitandDit. This perceptron does not have any estimated weights: its only purpose is to compute the functionminEquation (1). We design the network to extract thegandg+csemiparametrically, thus imposing the break atRit= 0 in (1) embedded in the theory of differential timeliness, without requiring other parametric assumptions. The input layer of the network encoding functionsfanddis a vector of dimensionncorresponding to the variablesYit, the intermediate layer uses hyperbolic tangent activation functions,10and the output layer consists of a single perceptron whose output is the value of the respective functions inFigure 2(a). As we do not impose restrictions on the values of𝑓⁡(.)f⁡(.)and𝑑⁡(.)d⁡(.), the activation function of their output perceptron is chosen to be linear, thus allowing for any real value. However, we impose as an economically intuitive restriction that earnings should be conditionally nondecreasing in returns, consistent with earnings indicating changes in net wealth and implying positive functionsgandg+c. To implement this, we use an exponential activation function for their output perceptron inFigure 2(b).11Because neural networks typically use stochastic optimizers, models trained with the same parameters may exhibit slight differences. To reduce noise and ensure replicability, we use the average of an ensemble of one hundred models trained with different seeds. 3.2. Data and Preliminaries We start with firm-year observations from both Compustat and CRSP databases with fiscal year ends from January 1, 1963, through December 31, 2019, for a total of 229,387 firm-year observations and 21,392 unique firms. For the full sample, we require nonmissing net income, share price, common outstanding shares, book equity, debt, and total assets. We further require a match to the CRSP database and nonmissing compounded returns over the previous eight months (to exclude the earnings announcement from the prior year) and drop all firm-year observations with a share price below$⁢1$⁢1. For measures using additional variables, we keep observations with nonmissing leverage, net operating accruals, cash flows from operations, investment cycle, volatility, and age, as defined inTable A.1, which shrinks the sample further to 185,581 firm-year observations and 17,645 unique firms. To improve comparability across years in pooled regressions, we deflate size by the consumer price index from the St Louis Federal Reserve.12All variables are winsorized by year at the 1% level. InTable 1, a median firm has a price to earnings of about 17 (1/0.0571/0.057), achieved a yearly return of 4.1%, has a size of$⁢70$⁢70billion (log 5.094), a market-to-book of 1.58, and an enterprise value that is about 0.23 in debt (0.3/(1+0.3)0.3/(1+0.3)). Table 1.Descriptive Statistics Table 1.Descriptive Statistics As benchmarks, we compute several standard measures of time-varying differential timeliness. The models L1 and L1y are ordinary least squares estimate of (1): In model L1, we define the variablesYitassize,M/B, andleverageand restrictgandcto be linear to create an index variable; model L1y corresponds to KW, wheregandcare assumed to have coefficients that depend on calendar time and the model is estimated with separate yearly regressions.13Models L2 and L2y contain five features examined in KW as associated to conservatism: volatility, net operating accruals, cash flow from operations, investment cycle, and firm age, as defined inTable A.1. Similar to models L1 and L1y, L2 includes only these variables, whereas L2y is estimated by year. Together, the four models L1, L1y, L2, and L2y are benchmarks achieved with standard linear regressions.14 To further motivate the benefits of a machine learning based measure, we note a potential drawback of the benchmark model KW (i.e., L1y). Given that the model is estimated with separate yearly regressions, the coefficients onsize,M/B, andleveragevary from year to year. As shown inFigure 3, the coefficients exhibit large spikes with only limited interpretable persistence that would map to trends in economic transactions or macroeconomic changes. In fact, all coefficients change sign over the sample period: TheM/Bcoefficient oscillates around zero and the coefficients onsizeandleveragecan change threefold year-over-year, occasionally becoming zero or slightly negative. Indeed, the time variation in coefficients contains most of the explanatory power of the model so that the averaged yearly coefficients do not fit the sample well: The full-sample adjustedR2using the average yearly coefficients drops from 26.5% to 6%; this seems intuitively surprising given that accounting practices are likely to be persistent. Specifically, if the true relation of interest is a complex function of firm characteristics that have changed over time, yearly linear regression with fewer characteristics or a misspecified functional form project the true relation as changes to yearly coefficients.15 Another diagnostic indicating noisy estimates is that the fitted relation between earnings and returns is often negative, which likely occurs only under unusual circumstances. We plot inFigure 4(first column) the density of the relation conditional on positive newsgand conditional on negative newsg+cin KW (L1y) and in a simpler model estimated with a single pooled regression (L1). In both cases, we expect the average effect to be such that earnings reflect information in the direction of the news, consistent with prior research on the relation between earnings and price (Ohlson 2001,Penman and Zhang 2002). However, in the KW measure,gis counterintuitively centered around zero, indicating that a random observation is about equally likely to exhibit lower or higher earnings conditional on higher returns. The large magnitudes of the coefficient (between −0.2 and .2) suggest that this feature is not due to a weak association between earnings and returns. Conditional on negative returns, observations are more likely to feature a positive association𝑔+𝑐>0g+c>0but, even then, roughly 11% of all observations feature a negative association. Given that the features used in KW are unlikely to capture the special circumstances leading to a negative association (early-stage growth firms with unusually high levels of investments in intangibles), the distribution of parameters suggests that the functionsgandg+cshould be restricted to remain positive.16 We develop four models estimated using a neural network approach by choosing the set of variablesYitand derive four measures of machine learning conservatism (MLC). The models MLC1 and MLC1y use the same three features as KW and are included to provide a direct comparison with L1y. In MLC1, we only include the three firm characteristics and assume that the relation does not change over time. In MLC1y, we allow for the relation to be different between years. Within a neural network approach, year-level variation can be incorporated by adding time fixed effects as additional features inYit. Unlike fixed effects in a pooled time series linear regression, the algorithm interacts these fixed effects to capture any time variation in coefficients of the model. Models MLC2 and MLC2y are identical to models L2 and L2y in terms of features but use the neural network. Overall, we derive eight different models, which can be compared pairwise over any of the following dimensions: (i) linear “L” versus machine learning “ML”, (ii) three “1” versus eight features “2,” and (iii) only economic features versus including time effects “y.” 3.3. Model Training Under the objective of using a model for prediction, a model is evaluated based on its performance out-of-sample. However, followingShmueli (2010)andKleinberg et al. (2015), our purpose in using machine learning is fundamentally different and aims to semiparametrically capture a pattern in the data. Hence, we train and evaluate the model differently from the traditional approach so that we can emphasize the ability of the model to capture the relationship of interest in (1). We tune the two fitting parameters of the neural network that have the greatest impact on the neural network: the number of iterations and the intermediate layer size. The number of iterations is the number of times the gradient of the loss function is computed and the weights are updated. The layer size refers to the number of perceptrons used in the intermediate layer of each embedded networkf,g,g+c, andd. This step is conceptually needed in a neural network because the network would always fit all the variation with enough perceptrons (Cybenko 1989) if the fitting parameters are set to maximize in-sample fit. Therefore, we cross-validate here as a statistical procedure to choose parameters in the model, similar to cross-validating the bandwidth with kernel density estimation (Pagan and Ullah 1999) or the regularization in the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator or ridge regression, given that these methods are commonly used for purposes other than prediction. This different purpose ties to structuring the cross-validation differently from a prediction exercise. The usual approach when machine learning is used for prediction is to divide the sample by year (Bao et al. 2020,Bertomeu et al. 2021), which reflects the prospective use of the model to predictdifferentyears and makes it more likely that the model performs well to future years overfitting specific aspects of the present. However, we are not predicting but fitting a nonlinear model whose functional form is unknown. Furthermore, comparability with existing measures estimated by year or with year fixed effects requires samples that haveallyears. For this reason, we conduct the cross-validation by sampling half of all the firms used for tuning the parameters (rather than sampling by year), train the model on the selected half, and compute its fit on a separate 30% of the data. The remaining 20% of the data forms our test sample and is discussed in the next section.17 3.4. Estimation and Goodness-of-Fit Once the parameters of the neural network are tuned, we train and evaluate the full model on the entire data set, which reflects that we seek to use all the information in the sample to estimate the relationship of interest. We proceed next to estimate the eight models: the linear models L1, L1y, L2, and L2y, and the neural networks MLC1, MLC1y, MLC2, and MLC2y, where models “2” refers to the inclusion of the additional features and models ending with “y” are estimated with separate yearly regressions (for linear models) or fixed effects (for neural networks). We compare these measures in the correlation matrix inTable 2. As expected, all measures are positively correlated, suggesting that they directionally capture a common construct. Machine learning measures tend to feature a correlation of 0.5 or above with their linear paired counterparts, providing a check that the first-order factors recovered in linear models are still present with the machine learning measures. Models without time fixed effects tend to be more correlated, which suggests that the inclusion of additional fixed effects may make the estimation noisier and capture less of a common component. Table 2.Pearson (Top) and Spearman (Bottom) Correlation Matrix Table 2.Pearson (Top) and Spearman (Bottom) Correlation Matrix Note.All correlations are statistically significant at the 5% level. We plot inFigure 4the distribution of measures across observations. Except for L1, linear measures tend to predict a level of conservatism centered around zero, whereas machine learning methods imply that most firms have positivec. The machine learning methods MLC2 and MLC2y feature less variation incalthough they allow for more flexible functional forms, possibly because the functional forms more accurately map income intogandcthan L1y, and remove variation that would cause violations of the positivity constraints ongandg+c.18The inclusion of additional variables in L2y corrects for most of the negative abnormal relation between earnings and returns conditional on positive returns in L1y, but it creates another anomaly, implying that about half of all observations have a negative relation conditional on positive returnsg+c. We report goodness-of-fit metrics inTable 3. To ensure comparability, we report the fit for the “1” and “2” models over the same finalized sample where the eight features are available.19We report the adjustedR2across linear models and the Akaike information criterion to account for differences in degrees of freedom across models (rows labeled “Full sample”). AlthoughR2metrics do not have a statistical foundation to compare nonlinear models, the AIC is designed to capture fit incorporating that certain models have more estimated parameters than others. Table 3.Model Fit Table 3.Model Fit Notes.All models are trained and measures of fit computed on the most restrictive sample where the eight features of models “2” are available. Rows labeled “full sample” report the fit on the full sample for model trained on the full sample. Rows labeled “Test sample” report the fit on 20% of the data for models trained on the remaining 80% of the data. The five additional features are critically important to explain the variation, as a simple linear regression L2 with only 36 coefficients to estimatef,g,c, anddand no time explains more variation than L1, L1y, and MLC1, and higher adjustedR2and lower AIC than all “1” models. MLC2 and MLC2y have a higherR2and lower AIC than other measures. However, the largest improvement in fit appears to be consistently driven by the inclusion of additional variables. For completeness, we also train the model on 80% randomly drawn observations and assess the fit in a test sample composed of the remaining 20% observations (rows labeled “Test sample”). The test sample was not used to train the model, which ensures that nonlinear models or models with more coefficients are not favored. Comparisons based onR2are very similar to the baseline. Because the AIC imposes a correction for overfitting, which is in principle not applicable to a test sample, it tends to favor models with fewer coefficients. Indeed, using this criterion, models with more estimated coefficients, such as MLC1y, L2y, and MLC2y, tend to perform worse relative to comparable models.
article.4. Validation: 4.1. Sampling Errors We assess the performance of these measures when confronted to a specific data-generating process (DGP). A common approach is to conduct simulations where the researcher defines a representative DGP and examines the behavior of measures when applied to a problem where the measure may be misspecified because of its assumptions or computational methodology. Any of these reasons will lead to statistical error in our setting, given that even the most general algorithms presume some regularities in the data and rely on a finite number of observations. As inBreuer and Schütt (2023)andJennings et al. (2023), we use simulations to assess errors in the empirical analysis. InTable 4, we block bootstrap by firm the features (Yit) of 100 new samples, construct simulated earnings from the model estimated in the earlier section, and add a random Gaussian noise term whose standard deviation is equal to the standard deviation of the residual of that model. Then, we simulate the process of a researcher who does not know the true DGP estimating the measure using their own (possibly incorrect) model. We rotate the true DGP and then run each measure against another DGP.20The mean absolute error (MAE) is the average absolute error between the truecand the estimated measure. Then, we average across all simulations and compute a standard error across the 100-point estimates of MAEs. Table 4.Model MAEs Table 4.Model MAEs Notes.Mean absolute errors between the C score from the true data-generating process and the estimated model, normalized by the standard deviation of the true DGP C score. Rows indicate the chosen estimation procedure, and columns indicate the true data-generating process. Every true data-generating processdis drawn 100 times (𝐶𝑠𝑖𝑚𝑑=𝐶𝑑+𝜖𝑑Cdsim=Cd+ϵd, whereCdis the C score estimated by the respective model andϵdfollows a centered normal distribution with a variance equal to the variance of the model’s residuals). The average mean absolute error of the 100 draws is tabulated. All averages are significant at the 1% level. For example, in column L1y, we reconstruct earningsXitfor each observation in the sample by using estimation L1y. Then, in row MLC2, the researcher runs the analysis and obtains a new MLC2-based C score for this particular sample; of course, the researcher is potentially misspecified and uses a model that has functional forms that are possibly too complex but ignores the time effects. A miscalibrated researcher using MLC2 when the true DGP is L1y would have a mean absolute error equal to 52.3% (half of a standard deviation of the true measure) versus 22.3% if (correctly) using L1y. Vice versa, if the true DGP is MLC2, the error is 49.6% using MLC2 versus 493% (or five standard deviations) using L1y. As is intuitive, estimation procedures do well in the diagonal because they are correctly specified for the true DGP. Errors are, unsurprisingly, larger when the process is more complex and includes nonlinearities or time effects. As to robustness, a better model can be read in rows and must be such that its errors are smaller than in other rows (i.e., comparing row vectors). All “1” models tend to perform poorly if the true DGP has more variables (columns for “2” models) and are often multiple standard errors away from the true value. MLC2 and MLC2y are the most robust approaches across models, with similar errors across all specifications (hovering at roughly half of a standard deviation). Also, MLC2y appears slightly less robust than MLC2: MLC2’s error against MLC2y remains at 45.1%, whereas MLC2y’s error against MLC2 is 80.9%, likely because MLC2y has many variables and incorrectly assigns the fit to time effects and their interactions. Across linear models, L2 is the most robust, with similar performance across all models except against MLC2 and MLC2y. The comparison with L1y reveals that this model works well mainly when it is the correct DGP, but otherwise features large MAEs, with mean absolute errors greater than one standard deviation against MLC1, L2, MLC2, and MLC2y. This suggests that a researcher would need a high degree of confidence that L1y is the true specification. Then, we simulate a generic statistical model in which a researcher uses the measure to test an economic theory but is not interested in the measure’s value as an end goal. We specify the test as follows: InTable 5, as expected, the measures still perform well in the diagonal. Throughout, all measures tend to feature attenuation bias given that they are measured with error. The attenuation bias is notable even for machine learning models, which suggests that MLC measures do not fully solve the problem of not having sufficient assumed economic structure to estimate measures precisely.21 Table 5.Estimated C Score Coefficients Table 5.Estimated C Score Coefficients Notes.Estimated from (2) with ordinary least squares using the average over 100 draws. Rows indicate the chosen estimation procedure and columns indicate the true data-generating process. Every true data-generating processdis drawn 100 times (𝐶𝑠𝑖𝑚𝑑=𝐶𝑑+𝜖𝑑Cdsim=Cd+ϵd, whereCdis the C score estimated by the respective model andϵdfollows a centered normal distribution with a variance equal to the variance of the model’s residuals). The average coefficient found with OLS on these draws tabulated. The C score coefficient used to generate the data (αinEquation (2)) is set equal to one. Theβcoefficients for size, M/B, and leverage are set to 4, −2, and 1, respectively. 4.2. Temporal Stability Linear C score measures have been criticized for their lack of temporal stability (Givoly et al. 2007). If measures based on machine learning capture firms’ conservatism, they should capture some inherent characteristics, such as corporate governance, leadership, organizational culture, or auditing procedures, many of which are highly persistent. Measures including time effects or separate regressions by year (“y” models) exhibit significant instability over time, whereas MLC2 can attain a reasonable fit without the instability caused by time effects; furthermore, along the lines ofGivoly et al. (2007), we explore later in this section the stability of the measure within firm. Figure 5plots time series changes in the construct of interest to examine long-term trends in accounting (Barth et al. 2023). Models L1, MLC1, L2, and MLC2 exhibit an increase until the mid 1970s, followed by a long-term trend of declining conservatism, starting in the decade of the 70 s and revealing that conservatism is currently near its lowest ever. To our knowledge, this pattern has not been empirically documented. However, it aligns with a gradual shift in conceptual statements, emphasizing relevance. This transition began with the initial conceptual statements introduced by the FASB and later evolved through a convergence with international accounting standards. The focus ultimately shifted toward neutrality and prudence, as described byZeff (2013). The models with yearly effects tend to reveal large variations in conservatism within short time spans and do not clearly show this trend expected from institutional facts, plausibly because the yearly coefficients are noisily estimated. We further report structural breaks followingBai and Perron (2003). All measures without time effects seem to more plausibly identify a sequence of breaks that would have decreased conservatism as part of the increased emphasis toward the asset-liability view. By contrast, L1y seems to identify a structural break around the creation of the FASB and then reveals higher conservatism. MLC1y and MLC2y also feature a second break in the early 1980s but, although conservatism is low after the break, it seems that the measure primarily captures greater stability starting after 1981 with average conservatism remaining relatively flat. To further document the presence of a trend, we report the results of a time series panel estimation controlling for the number of years since 1975. In this analysis, we standardize the measures by their unconditional standard deviation to facilitate comparisons of coefficients. We document inTable 6, consistent with the plots, that there is a general trend toward decreasing conservatism in most measures. However, this trend does not occur for L1y and MLC1y, and instead, we document a small positive trend. The most persistent measures, as captured by the coefficient on the lag, are the indexes L1 and L2 closely followed by MLC1, whereas the least persistent measure is L2y. The traditional L1y measure is more persistent than all “2” measures involving more factors except L2. Table 6.Trend Analysis Table 6.Trend Analysis Notes.This table estimates an auto-regressive time-series AR(1) model explaining the C score with a trend variable equal to the number of years since 1975. All C score measures are standardized, and Newest-West standard errors are used to account for autocorrelations. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. FollowingGivoly et al. (2007), we report inTable 7the autocorrelations of all measures between the current year and the previous year, two years before, and five years before. As expected, the inclusion of yearly fixed effects yields lower autocorrelations for both the linear and ML measures because these yearly shocks contain volatile information. Adding firm characteristics introduces more variation in the linear measures, and L2y autocorrelations are much lower than those of L1y, which makes L2y more problematic under this criterion. ML measures are more stable as additional variables are included, as supported by similar autocorrelations between MLC1 and MLC2. The additional firm characteristics combined with yearly fixed effects yield autocorrelations for MLC2y fairly close to or slightly higher than MLC2 autocorrelations, suggesting that these additional variables subsume the volatility in yearly shocks. Relative to L1y, MLC1 and MLC2 offer enhanced stability across various time horizons in terms of Spearman autocorrelations, but not Pearson correlations. However, in our context. Table 7.Autocorrelations Table 7.Autocorrelations Notes.This table shows the autocorrelations of all the measures between the current year and the previous year (first two rows), two years before (second and third rows), and five years before (last two rows). Spearman and Pearson correlations are tabulated and significant at the 5% level. Firms with missing years are excluded from this analysis. Next, as inGivoly et al. (2007), we report the proportions of firms that stayed in the same decile on different time horizons: one year later, two years later, and five years later inTable 8. A larger proportion of firms stay in the same decile with MLC1 and MLC2 relatively to L1y regardless of the time horizon. MLC1 and MLC2 feature the highest proportions of firms staying in the same decile for firms featuring very low or high levels of conservatism. The lowest proportions are for firms with moderate levels of conservatism (quantiles 3–5) ranging between 13% and 21% for L1y and between 16% and 35.3% for MLC1 and MLC2. As the time horizon widens, the proportion of firms staying within the same decile decreases. Table 8.Rank Analysis for All Measures Table 8.Rank Analysis for All Measures Notes.This table shows the fractions of firms remaining in the same C score deciles between the current year and the previous year (panel A), two years before (panel B), and five years before (panel C). Firms with missing years are excluded from this analysis. 4.3. Restatements We develop next a separate validation based onEttredge et al. (2012)to examine whether our ML measures can capture firms’ conservative behavior where it is expected. A natural setting is to study the restatement data in light of the information conveyed by the C-score measures. To conduct these tests, we pool the data from audit analytics from 2000 until 2019.22 We identify misstatements consistent with prior research (Srinivasan et al. 2015,Zakolyukina 2018,Cheynel and Zhou 2024) and remove frequent restatements that are economically innocuous or clear clerical errors (Hennes et al. 2008). To select observations more likely to capture accounting irregularities, we retain in Audit Analytics restatements that (i) trigger an investigation by the board of directors or the Securities and Exchange Commission or affect a revenue or core expense account as inZakolyukina (2018) and (ii) are income-decreasing. FollowingEttredge et al. (2012), we test whether the C-score measures detect higher conservatism in the years following the overstatement, as firms are expected to become less aggressive in the years following the detection of an irregularity. We define the post overstatement period as being of the same length as that of the misstatement it relates to.23 We conduct a test in means by comparing the C score during post periods to other periods, noting that greater conservatism in the post period should imply a positive difference. We consider two approaches to report this difference.Table 9, Panel A, displays the results in the original models, and Panel B uses retrained models that include the postmisstatement information as a feature. We find a significant positive difference for MLC2 and MLC2y in both panels. MLC1 and MLC1y do not perform as well: Their differences are insignificant using the original models and become positive and significant only in the retrained models. L2 and L2y yield positive and significant differences in both panels. Once the models are retrained (Panel B), the firm’s attributes are less important, and all MLC-score measures display a positive and significant difference. The magnitudes of the differences are, in general, larger in the retrained models, consistent with the machine learning algorithm training taking into account the restatement information. Table 9.C Score During Postrestatement Periods Table 9.C Score During Postrestatement Periods Notes.This table presents, for all measures, the difference between the C score for post core restatement periods and other periods between 2000 and 2019. In panel A, all models are the main models from the paper. In panel B, all models have been retrained and include the postrestatement dummy. We further conduct multivariate analyses inTable 10: We regress the C scores on post periods (dummy equal to one for the post overstatement periods) controlling for all firm characteristics used in models “2,” year fixed effects, and firm fixed effects to remove variations due to firms’ heterogeneity and time shocks. For the original models, MLC2 is the only measure that supports a significant positive coefficient at the 5% level on the post periods. The coefficient on the post periods L2 and L2y is insignificant, suggesting that the significant positive difference in means may be affected by yearly shocks or persistent firm characteristics. Once the models are retrained, all MLC-score measures and L2 and L2y yield a positive and significant coefficient on the post periods. Table 10.C Score During Postrestatement Periods Table 10.C Score During Postrestatement Periods Notes.This table regresses the C score on post core misstatement periods on different time periods. In panel A, all models are the main models from the paper. In panel B, all models have been retrained and include the post restatement dummy. All regressions include year and firm fixed effects, and control for the following firm characteristics: size, M/B, leverage, volatility, nonoperating accruals (NOAC), cash flows from operating activities (CFOA), investment cycle, and age. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01.
article.5. Concluding Remarks: Measuring characteristics of accounting information is notoriously difficult because one only observes the joint result of inherent economics and the choices made in the accounting system (Dechow et al. 2010). The problem of assessing conservatism combines all of these challenges: new transactions are continuously being recorded and affected by the economic environment. Isolating the effect of conservatism requires assessing whether these transactions are within the scope of conservative rules defined by GAAP, the level of verifiability and, more broadly, the level of certainty about future cash flows. The discretion that accounting policies offer in choices of conservatism can only be extracted after these economic factors have been satisfactorily included in the model. Machine learning is a statistical tool that, on its own, does not offer a complete model to solve this nontrivial identification challenge—presumably, the identification could only be resolved in principle via better consensus over the data-generating process of economic transactions in a structural model. However, machine learning may use observable economic variables more thoroughly to fit a flexible model that does not presume particular interactions or functional forms. Among observations worth emphasizing, we show that a machine learning model using an extended set of economic characteristics, but not a time-varying specification, can achieve a fit comparable to traditional linear methods with fewer estimated coefficients, appears more robust to misspecification, and reveals a secular decrease in conservatism. However machine learning methods do not achieve a fit that is necessarily much greater than a complex linear model involving many economic factors, and does not resolve the inherent limitations of the asymmetric timeliness measure. We conclude that machine learning measures are complements, rather than substitutes, to existing approaches, and should be of particular use when the researchers is interested in interactions and nonlinearities, or needs to remove noise due to yearly variation. The analysis is part of a broader research agenda showing that machine learning tools can be used beyond prediction tasks. Refined algorithms may provide new methods to filter noise when the relation between signal and noise is not fully captured by a closed-form statistical model. Most theories do not fully specify the correct empirical research design, and researchers may argue about functional forms, interactions, or variable inclusions. We illustrate this objective by incorporating machine learning into differential timeliness and present an approach that can be adapted to incorporate additional determinants or controls (Ball et al. 2013b,Frankel et al. 2019,Badia et al. 2021). A similar approach may be considered to enhance other noisy proxies, such as accounting quality (Barth et al. 2023), cost of capital (Lambert et al. 2007,2012;Bertomeu and Cheynel 2016), or accruals (Dechow et al. 1995,Armstrong et al. 2016). We hope that the methods can be assessed more broadly to examine whether they improve commonly used empirical measurements. The authors thank seminar participants at INSEAD, the Columbia Accounting Design Workshop, the University of Hawai’i, and the Universidad Carlos III, as well as two anonymous reviewers and the associate editor, for valuable comments.

We augment by including volatility, non-operating accruals, cash flows from operating activities, investment cycle and age. 3Neither Hicksian income nor the concepts statement are authoritative, and some rules contradict these principles (e.g., expensing R&D and marketing or excluding contingent liabilities that cannot be reasonably estimated or unlikely); however, when many transactions are aggregated into accounting income, higher income usually indicates higher valuation (Ohlson 2001,Penman and Zhang 2002), especially given that our estimates are conditional expectations on variables that would not naturally point to special contexts in which lower income could be associated to good news. 4This literature focuses on the analysis of stakeholders and the doctrines presented in academic research, concept statements, and practice. To oversimplify, the asset-liability view does not rule out conservatism, but it removes from the core doctrine a number of properties that would usually increase differential timeliness. For example, as documented inOrthaus et al. (2023), the FASB and IASB excluded conservatism or prudence from the set of desirable qualitative characteristics, which itself followed decades of changes that greatly reduced its importance in guiding new standards. 5There are other studies investigating machine learning in accounting, and our objective here is not to provide a comprehensive discussion; the recent surveys byBertomeu (2020)andKrupa and Minutti-Meza (2022)provide a discussions of new perspectives in the accounting literature. 6These examples are not intended as a complete list of current work in this area; recent studies also includeJones (2017),Anand et al. (2019),Chen et al. (2022),Lee et al. (2020), andHunt et al. (2021). 7Differential timeliness is a workhorse model to test theories of conservatism (Ball et al. 2000,2003;Givoly and Hayn 2000;Lafond and Roychowdhury 2008). 8There is no standard framework for the empirical measurement of conservatism that nests all approaches. For example, another distinction is that conservatism can be “unconditional,” in the form of a reduction in the value of assets regardless of economic news, versus “conditional,” a reduction that depends on the nature of economic events. From an information economics perspective, unconditional changes in a measure do not change the properties of the signal, so we focus here on definitions that preserve some informational role (Gigler et al. 2009). 9Other algorithms, such as regression trees, are very effective at tasks that involve the prediction of a single object without any assumed structure, but imposing a semi-parametric structure, although possible, is not as straightforward as designing a customized neural network. 10The hyperbolic tangenttanh⁡(𝑥)tanh⁡(x), allows for the output of the perceptron to lie between −1 and 1 and its derivative is well defined on the real line. It is one of the traditional activation function used in neural networks. The outputs of all the perceptrons of the intermediate layers are inputs to the output layer. Using a nonlinear activation function for the intermediate layer perceptron is what gives the network the ability to fit nonlinear functional forms. 11Using an exponential activation function forces the output to be positive. One could in principle use any positive activation function to that end. The fit of the model increases if we do not impose positivity restrictions and the other validations perform similarly, as shown in the online appendix. We use these restrictions because one benefit of semiparametric approaches is that they allow the researcher to more easily incorporate theoretically motivated constraints without a somewhat ad hoc adjustment to linearity that would create a mass of “zeros” that does not fit the problem well (such as imposing a lower bound at zero on a linear form). We do not restrict conservatismcto be positive, and some firms exhibit negative conservatism. For purposes of interpretation, it has been observed in the literature that “zero” asymmetric timeliness need not mean neutral accounting (Beaver and Ryan 2005,Givoly et al. 2007,Breuer and Windisch 2019); rather,cis intended as a proxy such that highercmeans more conservative. 12We use the same data construction steps asKhan and Watts (2009), including for the choice of an eight-month window, to ensure comparability and reduce discretion, except that we use a longer time window. The definitions of the variables and data selection are detailed inTables A.1andA.2. 13Model L1y is the most common empirical specification of the measure (Goh and Li 2011,Ettredge et al. 2012,Jayaraman 2012,Ahmed and Duellman 2013,Tan 2013,Martin and Roychowdhury 2015,DeFond et al. 2016,Kim and Zhang 2016,Lara et al. 2016,Huang and Hilary 2018). 14In the online appendix, we further establish comparability to prior studies by comparing the coefficient estimates in our full sample, model L1y, relative to the coefficients inTable 3of KW. For completeness, we also replicate their research design within their sample period and recover very similar coefficients. The coefficients are similar in magnitude to the original estimates; more recent periods after their sample ends exhibit conservatism with lesser sensitivity to market-to-book (coefficient𝐷×𝑅𝑒𝑡×𝑀/𝐵D×Ret×M/B) and greater sensitivity to size and leverage (coefficients𝐷×𝑅𝑒𝑡×𝑆𝑖𝑧𝑒D×Ret×Sizeand𝐷×𝑅𝑒𝑡×𝐿𝑒𝑣D×Ret×Lev). 15To gain intuition, suppose firms are observed over two consecutive years with an increase in leverage from one to two and there exists a (true) functional form on conservatism that is convex. The machine learning model will approximate the convex form, while the linear model estimated by year will fit the data with different yearly coefficients. This need not make the measure in the linear model more volatile, but suppose next that only a fraction of the firms in the sample increased from one to two, with all other firms staying identical. The linear model must attribute the new model parameters to all firms, which may cause a greater joint variation in the aggregate level of conservatism, whereas, in fact, the linear projection into year fixed effects and their interaction is noisier. 16We adopt here the view that these restrictions are generally believed to be true from prior literature, so including them as an overidentifying restriction will in principle increase the accuracy of the measure. However, our results are qualitatively unchanged and typically stronger if we do not impose positivity. In the online appendix, we show that theR2increases from 0.478 (0.523) to 0.499 (0.553) for MLC2 (MLC2y). 17To reduce sensitivity to outliers, we use a Huber loss𝐿𝛿⁡(𝑎)Lδ⁡(a)withδ= 1 (𝐿𝛿⁡(𝑎)Lδ⁡(a)equals12⁢𝑎212⁢a2for|𝑎|<𝛿|a|<δand𝛿⁢(|𝑎|−12⁢𝛿)δ⁢(|a|−12⁢δ)otherwise) when training the neural network as opposed to the mean-squared errors, which tend to be sensitive to outliers in models like ours with few features. To reduce the complexity of the search, we constrain all networks (𝑓,𝑔,𝑔+𝑐f,g,g+candd) to have the same number of perceptrons in the intermediate layer. We did not find benefits to using more than one layer and, in theory, a single layer can fit any functional form (in complex problems with unstructured data, for example, image processing, it has been nevertheless shown that multiple layers outperform a single layer). We also cross-validated on the learning rate and found similar results. We use the Adam optimizer with the default parameters of the tensorflow library (learning rate = 0.001,𝛽1=0.9,𝛽2=0.999β1=0.9,β2=0.999, and𝜖=1×10−7ϵ=1×10−7). The cross-validation indicates the following hyperparameters (iterations; layer size): MLC1 (12,000; 25), MLC1y (4,000; 40), MLC2 (10,000; 40), MLC2y (3,500; 40). 18For completeness, we ran the neural networks algorithm without positivity constraints. In the online appendix, we report the fit and plot the densities. Given that these models do not impose any restrictions ongorg+c, the training sample fit is slightly higher by a few percent: an increase ranging between 2.4% and 3.4%. The fit for the test sample is almost identical, only increasing by less than 0.5%. The differences between the distribution of the measures with and without positivity constraints are more pronounced for the ML measures incorporating additional variables. 19A potential concern when using the larger sample for the fit “1” measures is that many firms included in this extended sample with missing items are more unusual. In the online appendix, we document that the comparisons are qualitatively unchanged when evaluating the fit on the full sample. All measures inTable 3were trained on the restricted sample. 20We calibrate the DGP as if the estimates of the model were the actual true process. Of course, these estimates contain error and need not be an exact match of the process; therefore, these results only speak to the desirability of a method if an underlying process is either more or less complex than the method assumes. To keep the analysis as transparent as possible, we do not cross-validate the machine learning models for each DGP but use the parameters of our baseline cross-validation. Cross-validation is the time-consuming part of the analysis and an objective of our study is to offer reasonable hyperparameters that can be easily re-used to recover measures of conservatism. 21In the online appendix, we examine type II errors defined as a false acceptance of no effect, and look at the ability of the models to correctly sign the parameterα. We find MLC2 and MLC2y to be the most robust at that exercise. We also look at type I errors and assess the probability that the models incorrectly rejectα= 0. Specifically, we simulateEquation (2)withαset to zero. All models perform well at this task, and cases in which an effect is found despite no effect in the true DGP are the exception rather than the norm. 22We identify “core” misstatements following the earlier literature (Palmrose and Scholz 2004,Zakolyukina 2018,Cheynel and Zhou 2024); these misstatements are more likely related to frauds and ongoing investigations, relative to all misstatements, many of which are economically innocuous or due to clerical errors (Hennes et al. 2008). Summary statistics are given inTable A.3. Our objective here is not to replicateEttredge et al. (2012), as we use a different period and a narrower selection of misstatements inPalmrose and Scholz (2004)andZakolyukina (2018). 23Another possibility would be to consider the relation between misstatement “occurrence” and conservatism; however, this prediction is theoretically ambiguous (Bertomeu et al. 2017) because a more conservative system increases potential rewards for high ex post performance. Furthermore, even if conservatism were to directly reduce misstatements all other things equal, it is possible that firms at greater risk of misstatements would endogenously be more conservative or that more conservative firms detect a greater portion of misstatements.
